Given that MigrainePredict is a wearable healthcare AI system, an ongoing scientific study involving it could reasonably fall under 'testing in real world conditions'.
To determine if keeping the dataset is permitted, there must be a legal provision that allows for an exception to the right to erasure.

The scenario describes MigrainePredict, a high-risk AI system dealing with special-category health data, automatically suspending a user's access to an insurance-linked discount based solely on its predictions, with no human involvement, and producing legal effects for the user.
The use of sensitive health data to automatically impose a financial penalty without human oversight can be considered an "exploitative AI-enabled practice" that causes significant financial harm.
Therefore, subjecting the user to this decision is prohibited.
The MigrainePredict system is classified as a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act Annex III.
This directly violates the obligation set forth in aiact:art_9/par_2.
Personal data must be deleted when it is no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which it was collected or processed.
Health data is a special category of personal data.
Identifiable sensor data constitutes special categories of personal data (health data).
The data constitutes special categories of personal data.
The end of the retention period is implied when there is no longer a purpose for its processing.
Withdrawing consent could lead to a requirement for data deletion if consent was the legal basis for processing.
The user's demand for erasure of personal data typically falls under the 'right to be forgotten' as outlined in GDPR Article 17.
Paragraph 1 of GDPR Article 17 establishes the conditions under which a data subject has the right to demand erasure.
Paragraph 3 of GDPR Article 17 outlines exceptions to this right, including for scientific research purposes.
The right to withdraw consent (mentioned in [gdpr:art_7/par_3]) can be a basis for erasure.
To determine if the company must comply with this deletion request, the provisions of the right to erasure (GDPR Article 17) would typically be consulted.
The right to object is distinct from the right to erasure.
MigrainePredict cannot rely on the exemption from keeping records of processing activities as outlined in GDPR Article 30(5).
'Regularly' means the processing is not occasional.
'Health data' is a special category of data under GDPR Article 9(1).
Both conditions that lift the exemption (processing not occasional, includes special categories of data) are met.
MigrainePredict is obligated to keep records of its processing activities.
The GDPR's data-processing obligations apply exclusively to the processing of 'personal data'.
Data that has been fully anonymised to the extent that it cannot be linked back to an identifiable natural person is no longer considered personal data under the GDPR.
The GDPR's data-processing obligations do not govern the publication of such data.
When data is truly anonymised, it no longer constitutes personal data.
Therefore, the GDPR's data-processing obligations do not apply to such data.
As such, no retrieved requirement governs this scenario.
When data is truly anonymised to the extent that it can no longer be linked to an identified or identifiable natural person, it ceases to be personal data under the GDPR.
Therefore, the obligations of the GDPR, which are predicated on the processing of personal data, do not apply to such anonymised data.
Since the data in question is no longer personal data, no retrieved requirement governs this scenario.
